question: Compare the documented XML representations of an escalation boundary catch event and an escalation boundary catch-all event. Which attribute is present on the regular catch event but intentionally omitted on the catch-all variant?

a.text: The regular escalation boundary catch event carries `escalationRef="Escalation_2alpsjo"` on `bpmn:escalationEventDefinition`; the catch-all variant omits that attribute so it matches all thrown escalations.

b.text: `attachedToRef="Activity_1q7i1lv"` appears on both XML examples; it identifies the activity the boundary event is attached to, not catch-all behaviour.

c.text: `cancelActivity="false"` appears on both XML examples; it controls interruption behaviour, not whether the event is catch-all.

d.text: Both XML examples include an `id` on `bpmn:escalationEventDefinition`; the `id` is an XML identifier and is unrelated to catch-all behaviour.

explanation: In the documented XML, the only difference between the regular escalation boundary catch event and the catch-all variant is the presence or absence of `escalationRef` on `bpmn:escalationEventDefinition`. Omitting `escalationRef` makes the boundary event catch all thrown escalations.

codeBlocks.stem:
<!-- Regular escalation boundary catch event -->
<bpmn:boundaryEvent id="Event_1wpcmdz" cancelActivity="false"
    attachedToRef="Activity_1q7i1lv">
  <bpmn:escalationEventDefinition id="EscalationEventDefinition_1fpge5i"
      escalationRef="Escalation_2alpsjo" />
</bpmn:boundaryEvent>

<!-- Escalation boundary catch-all event -->
<bpmn:boundaryEvent id="Event_1wpcmdz" cancelActivity="false"
    attachedToRef="Activity_1q7i1lv">
  <bpmn:escalationEventDefinition id="EscalationEventDefinition_1fpge5i" />
</bpmn:boundaryEvent>
